history: Rachel has a longstanding pattern of relationship difficulties with her husband Ivan and experiences significant work-related stress. Her father’s successful career and strong work ethic have shaped her high self-expectations, contributing to her current distress, especially after being laid off. She tends to immerse herself in intellectual activities, particularly mathematics, as a way to feel competent and in control. Her sister Emma often takes a caring, possibly protective, role, which may influence Rachel’s tendency to downplay her vulnerabilities and assert independence through achievement. Rachel avoids discussing emotional issues directly, preferring to focus on structured, logical pursuits.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
I am bound to be rejected
intermediate_belief: If I do not succeed or meet high standards, I will be seen as a failure and others will think less of me. Achieving mastery and understanding is the only way I can prove my worth and avoid feeling inadequate. If I show vulnerability or depend on others, I risk being seen as weak or incapable.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I keep failing to secure a job, it means I am fundamentally incapable and things will never improve for me. No matter how hard I try, I will always fall short of what is expected, so there is no point in hoping for better outcomes.
coping_strategies: Rachel immerses herself in intellectual activities, such as mathematics and tutoring, to regain a sense of competence and control when distressed. She avoids discussing or confronting emotional pain, particularly regarding her job loss and relationship issues, by focusing on structured, logical pursuits. She seeks reassurance through mastery of complex concepts, using achievement as a buffer against feelings of inadequacy.
situation: Received a job rejection email while searching for work.
auto_thought: I failed again—this just proves I'm not good enough. No matter how hard I try, I can't measure up.
behavior: She immediately turns her attention to working on math problems and tutoring, spending extra time on intellectual tasks. She avoids talking about her feelings regarding the rejection and does not share her distress with her husband or sister.
